1: The rebellion is introduced with a genealogy: Korah son of Izhar son of Kohath son of Levi. Korah is a Kohathite Levite — from the highest-status Levitical family, the one tasked with carrying the ark and the most holy objects (Num 4:4-15). His revolt is not from the margins but from within the established liturgical structure. Dathan and Abiram are Reubenites — a significant coalition: the premier Levitical clan joins with descendants of Jacob's firstborn. The rebellion has both priestly-class and firstborn-tribe dimensions.
2: The 250 are not a mob but Israel's civic elite: nesi'ei edah (leaders of the congregation), qri'ei mo'ed (called/invited to the assembly — the formal term for covenant representatives), anshei shem (men of name/renown). This is an organized challenge by Israel's leadership class. In ANE contexts, high-status temple officials or civic nobles sometimes challenged priestly appointments when they felt their prerogatives were being exceeded. The coalition represents a structural challenge to Moses's and Aaron's combined political-religious authority.
3: 'The whole congregation is holy, every one of them, for the LORD is in their midst' — the challenge's theological framing. The claim is not false: Exod 19:6 does call Israel a 'kingdom of priests' and a 'holy nation.' But Korah's company draws an illegitimate conclusion: that universal holiness eliminates the need for differentiated priestly roles. The argument is a misapplication of a genuine theological truth. In the ANE, the tension between royal appointment of priests (as in Egypt and Mesopotamia) and hereditary priestly privilege was well-known; Korah's challenge parallels moments when non-priestly elites attempted to usurp cultic functions.
4: Moses's response to the challenge is to fall facedown — prostration before YHWH, not before the rebels. The posture signals two things simultaneously: submission to YHWH's judgment (which Moses is confident will vindicate him) and the gravity of what is happening. Moses does not rebuke Korah immediately with words; he falls before YHWH. The narrative models the proper response to illegitimate authority challenges: not human self-defense but deference to divine judgment.
5: 'Tomorrow morning YHWH will show who is his and who is holy, and will bring that one near to himself.' The censer test is postponed to the next morning — the delay serves both narrative and theological purposes. Israel must wait on YHWH's timing rather than seeking an immediate resolution. The phrase asher yivchar bo yaqriv elav — 'the one he chooses he will bring near' — recapitulates the central vocabulary of priestly election: bachar (choose), qarav (draw near). Priestly proximity to YHWH is a matter of divine choice, not human qualification or claim.
6: Moses instructs Korah's company to prepare censers (machtot) — the bronze fire-pans used for transporting incense coals. The censer was intimately associated with the most sacred moment of the liturgical year: the high priest's incense-burning inside the Holy of Holies on Yom Kippur (Lev 16:12-13), where the incense cloud was meant to screen the priest from YHWH's presence. By inviting the 250 to bring censers, Moses is creating a test that will expose them to the direct divine encounter for which they are not authorized — and for which they are not prepared.
7: 'The man YHWH chooses shall be the holy one' — the censer test is a divine oracle, not a human competition. The test is elegant in its simplicity: YHWH does not need to argue; he will simply show. Rav lakhem b'nei levi — 'you have gone too far, sons of Levi' — Moses turns the rebels' own phrase back on them (v.3 uses rav lakhem against Moses; now Moses uses it against Korah). The reversal is pointed: the accusation of excess applies to the accusers themselves.
8: 'Hear now, sons of Levi' — Moses addresses Korah and the Levites directly and separately from Dathan and Abiram. The Levitical challenge is distinct from the Reubenite challenge: Korah wants priestly access; Dathan and Abiram challenge Mosaic political authority (vv.12-14). Moses addresses each grievance on its own terms. The direct appeal to Korah as a Levite acknowledges the legitimacy of Levitical service even as Moses denies the legitimacy of Korah's further ambitions.
9: 'Is it too small a thing for you that the God of Israel has separated you from the congregation of Israel to bring you near to himself?' — Moses articulates what the Levitical vocation actually is: havdil (to separate, distinguish), qarav (to draw near). The Levites have already been brought near to YHWH — nearer than any other tribe. They serve the tabernacle, they bear the holy vessels, they stand between Israel and YHWH's holiness. To count this as a small thing is to despise the very gift of nearness that YHWH has given them.
10: 'He has brought you near, and all your fellow Levites with you. And yet you seek the priesthood too?' The phrase uvikashtem gam kehunah — 'you are also seeking the priesthood' — identifies the specific object of Korah's ambition. The priesthood (kehunah) is distinct from Levitical service: the priests burn incense, enter the sanctuary, eat the most holy portions. The Kohathites' proximity to the most holy objects had apparently whetted rather than satisfied their desire for priestly access. Moses diagnoses ambition masquerading as egalitarian theology.
11: 'Therefore it is against the LORD that you and all your company have gathered.' Moses cuts to the theological core: the rebellion is not against him personally but against YHWH's appointment. Ki veHoshem attem noalim — 'for what is Aaron, that you grumble against him?' Moses depersonalizes the attack: he and Aaron are not the targets; YHWH's structure is. This is a significant theological move: attacks on legitimate authority in Israel are attacks on the God who appointed that authority. The challenge is ultimately theocentric, not political.
12: Moses summons Dathan and Abiram for a separate confrontation — but they refuse to come. 'We will not come up' — lo na'aleh. The refusal to come before Moses is itself an act of rebellion: in Israel's covenantal structure, summoning before the covenant mediator was equivalent to a divine summons. Their refusal is not just impolite; it is a statement that they no longer recognize Moses's authority to summon them at all.
13: Dathan and Abiram's accusation is remarkable: they describe Egypt as 'a land flowing with milk and honey' — the phrase YHWH uses throughout the wilderness narrative for Canaan. They have reversed the theological geography: Egypt (the land of bondage) is reimagined as the place of abundance; Canaan (the promised land) is the threat of death. This is a grotesque inversion — a measure of how far the rebels' perception has drifted from YHWH's framing of the exodus. The ANE context of slavery in Egypt, with its Nile agriculture, may lie behind the romanticization: Egypt's irrigated fields were genuinely fertile.
14: 'You have not brought us into a land flowing with milk and honey, nor have you given us fields and vineyards.' The accusation is technically true — Israel is still in the wilderness. But the force of the covenant promise has not been rescinded; Israel's situation is a consequence of their own faithlessness at Kadesh (Num 13-14). Dathan and Abiram suppress this context. They also accuse Moses of seeking to 'rule over us' — ki tishtarer aleinu — delegitimizing his authority entirely. The charge is that the exodus was a leadership project serving Moses's ambitions, not YHWH's salvation.
15: Moses's anger is expressed through prayer: 'Do not accept their offering.' Al tefan el-minchatam — do not turn toward their grain offering. The imprecation echoes Cain and Abel (Gen 4:3-5): YHWH's acceptance or rejection of an offering is the final verdict on the offerer. Moses then provides his own defense: 'I have not taken one donkey from them, nor have I harmed any one of them.' The list of potential abuses he has NOT committed defines the standard of legitimate leadership: a leader who has not extracted wealth or done personal harm. The defense anticipates Samuel's similar declaration in 1 Sam 12:3.
16: The confrontation is scheduled for the next day before YHWH — Moses, Aaron, Korah, and the 250 all appearing at the tent of meeting. The divine presence is the intended arbiter: YHWH will adjudicate through the censer test itself. The staging is significant: the tent of meeting is the site of covenant encounter, and the confrontation is being brought formally into YHWH's presence rather than adjudicated by human authority. The test will be a liturgical act, resolved by divine fire.
17: Each of the 250 takes a censer — 250 censers plus Moses's and Aaron's. The scale is specified deliberately: 250 unauthorized censers vs. Aaron's single legitimate one. In ancient Israelite practice, the number of censers in use before YHWH was tightly controlled; unauthorized censers had precedent in Nadab and Abihu (Lev 10:1-2), who offered 'strange fire' and were consumed. The 250 are effectively reenacting that transgression at massive scale.
18: The 250 and Korah assemble at the entrance of the tent of meeting with fire in their censers and incense on them — the prescribed liturgical form. They have done everything correctly except one thing: they are not authorized. The narrative emphasizes their technical compliance to highlight the distinction between form and authorization. In the ANE, unauthorized individuals performing legitimate cultic forms were sometimes punished harshly by temples; the issue was not the correctness of the ritual but the legitimacy of the practitioner.
19: 'Korah gathered the whole congregation against them at the entrance of the tent of meeting.' The rebellion reaches its climax: Korah has organized the entire assembly into opposition. Then YHWH's glory appears to the whole congregation. The kavod YHWH (the glory of the LORD) is the visible manifestation of divine presence — a theophanic phenomenon associated with the cloud and fire of the tabernacle. Its appearance is the divine answer to the assembly's challenge: YHWH himself arrives to adjudicate.
20: YHWH's first response to the theophany is a command to Moses and Aaron: separate yourselves from this congregation. The command mirrors YHWH's instruction at Sodom (Gen 18:20-19:12) and the Korah account's earlier parallel with the golden calf episode (Exod 32:9-10), where YHWH instructs Moses to stand aside while judgment falls. The pattern of divine wrath requiring a righteous remnant's separation is a consistent feature of YHWH's judicial acts throughout Israel's wilderness narrative.
21: 'Separate yourselves from this congregation so that I may consume them in an instant.' Rega' — in a moment, instantaneously. The same word appears in Exod 33:5 and Num 17:45 — YHWH's capacity for instantaneous judgment is a theological marker of his absolute sovereignty. The threat to consume the entire congregation (not just Korah's company) reflects how seriously YHWH takes communal solidarity with rebellion: those who passively acquiesce to a challenge to divine authority share in its guilt.
22: Moses and Aaron fall facedown and intercede: 'O God, God of the spirits of all flesh' — the same title used in Num 27:16 (Moses's prayer for a successor) — 'shall one man sin and you be angry with the whole congregation?' The intercession appeals to YHWH's justice: corporate punishment for individual sin violates the principle of proportional judgment. Significantly, both the rebel Korah and the intercessors Moses and Aaron use prostration as their posture before YHWH. But the meaning differs: Korah's company prostrates in a liturgical act; Moses and Aaron prostrate in intercessory prayer.
23: YHWH responds to the intercession — a partial modification: the whole congregation will not be destroyed, but the specific rebels' households will be targeted. The divine response to Moses and Aaron's intercession is not total capitulation but a narrowing of judgment scope. The pattern of covenantal intercession modifying divine judgment (cf. Gen 18:22-33; Exod 32:11-14; Amos 7:1-6) recurs throughout Israel's relationship with YHWH: the mediator's prayer genuinely affects the scope of divine action.
24: 'Tell the congregation to move away from the dwellings of Korah, Dathan, and Abiram.' The separation of innocent from guilty is enacted spatially: physical distance from the rebels' tents is required. The instruction presupposes that proximity to judgment is dangerous — contact with those under divine curse is itself a risk. This is the spatial-moral logic of purity and contagion applied to divine judgment: the rebels' tents become zones of danger that must be evacuated.
25: Moses goes in person to Dathan and Abiram — they would not come to him (v.12), so he goes to them. The elders of Israel accompany Moses, giving his action communal authority. Moses's willingness to approach those who have rebelled against him and refused to appear before him is itself a form of leadership: he brings the judgment to them rather than allowing their refusal to stall the process. The elders as witnesses ensure that the judgment, when it comes, will be publicly credible.
26: 'Move away from the tents of these wicked men' — anshei harasha'im ha'eleh — 'and touch nothing that belongs to them, lest you be swept away in all their sins.' The verb tissafu (be swept away) is the same root used of being gathered to one's people at death (Num 27:13) and of divine collection-for-judgment. To touch the rebels' possessions is to be caught up in the sphere of their guilt. The prohibition has ANE parallels in devotion (cherem) practice, where the possessions of those under divine curse were themselves cursed and could not be touched without contamination.
27: The congregation separates; Dathan and Abiram stand defiantly at the entrances of their tents — with their wives, children, and infants. The detail is theologically difficult: the families are present and will be consumed. Ancient Israelite judicial practice sometimes extended corporate punishment to households for the head's crime (cf. Achan, Josh 7:24-26). The theological rationale is not that the children were guilty but that the rebellion's consequences fell on the entire social unit the rebel led. The families' presence also intensifies the tragedy of the coming judgment.
28: 'By this you shall know that the LORD has sent me to do all these works, and that it has not been of my own accord.' The coming judgment is explicitly framed as a divine sign vindicating Moses's prophetic commission. Ki lo millibi — 'for it was not from my heart.' Moses is not seeking personal vindication; he is seeking YHWH's vindication. The formula 'you shall know that YHWH has sent me' connects to the Exodus yada' theme: YHWH acts in history so that Israel (and others) may know him as the acting God.
29: If the rebels die a natural death — kmot kol ha'adam, the common death of all humanity — then Moses was not sent by YHWH. This is a falsifiability test: Moses is publicly staking his prophetic authority on a specific, observable outcome. The willingness to set up a verifiable test is itself a feature of authentic prophecy in Israel (Deut 18:21-22). The contrast with ordinary death creates anticipation: something other than ordinary death is about to occur.
30: 'But if the LORD creates something entirely new' — uvri'ah YHWH beriah. The word beri'ah (a creation, something uniquely brought into existence) derives from bara (H1254, the word for God's creative act in Gen 1:1). What is about to happen is not natural but genuinely new: a theophanic creation event. The earth will open its mouth and swallow them — they will go down alive into Sheol. This is both a geological-physical event and a theological statement: YHWH's judgment penetrates beneath the ordered surface of creation into the realm of death itself.
31: 'As soon as he finished speaking all these words, the ground under them split apart.' The immediacy of fulfillment — the ground opens the moment Moses finishes speaking — validates the prophetic word in the most compressed possible way. There is no delay between the spoken word and the event: YHWH's action is coterminous with Moses's speech. This pattern (word → immediate fulfillment) is the signature of prophetic authority in Israel, and its extreme compression here marks the event as a definitive divine verdict.
32: The earth swallows Korah, Dathan, Abiram, their households, and all the people belonging to Korah. The scope of the judgment is the household — the basic social unit of Israelite life. The phrase et kol ha'adam asher l'korah — 'all the people who belonged to Korah' — may refer to Korah's extended household network or followers. The Sheol-descent of the living is without direct ANE parallel in its theological specificity: these rebels do not merely die; they are actively received into the realm of the dead while still alive.
33: 'They went down alive into Sheol, they and all who belonged to them. The earth closed over them, and they perished from the midst of the assembly.' Sheol (H7585) — the realm of the dead in Hebrew thought: a dim underworld of shades, separated from YHWH's presence (cf. Ps 6:5; 88:3-6; Isa 38:18). The text does not describe Sheol's geography but its theological character: it is separation from the covenant community and from YHWH. For the rebels to enter it alive is for their judgment to be both physical and theological — a literal descent into the consequence of separation from YHWH that they had been living in spiritual form.
34: 'All the Israelites around them fled at their cry, for they said: Lest the earth swallow us also!' The community's terror is not cowardice but theological recognition: YHWH's judgment has demonstrated his holiness in the most dramatic possible way. The flight ensures there are survivors to witness and transmit the event. The cry of the people — pen tivcla'enu ha'aretz — mirrors the rebels' own fate and makes explicit what Israel has learned: the earth is an instrument of YHWH's justice that can consume the living.
35: 'Fire also came out from the LORD and consumed the 250 men who were offering incense.' A separate judgment for the separate component of the rebellion: while Dathan and Abiram are swallowed (the Reubenite-political dimension), the 250 are consumed by fire (the priestly-liturgical dimension). The divine fire recalls Nadab and Abihu (Lev 10:2: 'fire came out from before the LORD and consumed them'). The censers they carried — instruments of the unauthorized incense offering — survive to become the altar covering of vv.38-40.
36: YHWH immediately addresses the aftermath: the censers must be retrieved from among the burning. The distinction is notable — the fire from YHWH consumed the men but left the censers, which are now described as holy (v.37-38) — they have been brought near to YHWH, however illegitimately, and now belong to the sacred sphere. The sacred cannot simply be discarded; it must be reabsorbed into the sanctuary system appropriately.
37: Eleazar the priest (not Aaron — Aaron was involved in stopping the plague, v.46) is commanded to retrieve the censers. The coals must be scattered away from the site — the fire itself was holy. The censers are to be hammered (ritzu) into flat sheets — a metallurgical act of transformation. The instruments of rebellion become, through the priest's action, material for the sanctuary. The process mirrors Israel's broader theological pattern: what was used for transgression can be repurposed for covenantal service.
38: 'The censers of these men who sinned at the cost of their own lives' — benapshot'am — 'at the price of their lives.' The phrase underscores that the 250 paid with their lives for their act. The censers are hammered into flat sheets to overlay the altar. The altar covering becomes a permanent zikkaron (memorial/reminder) — a physical inscription in metal of the lesson: no unauthorized person shall approach to offer incense before YHWH. The bronze becomes a public teaching tool built into the structure of the sanctuary itself.
39: Eleazar carries out the command: he takes the bronze censers and has them hammered into altar plating. The execution is precise and immediate — as with all Mosaic commands, completion is noted. The censers have thus completed a strange journey: Levitical instruments used in an unauthorized priestly act, destroyed by divine fire, now permanently affixed to the altar as a warning against the very act they were used for. The altar itself becomes a commentary on the rebellion it survived.
40: 'A reminder to the Israelites that no unauthorized person — not of Aaron's line — should come near to offer incense before the LORD, so that he would not become like Korah and his company' — ka'asher dibber YHWH b'yad moshe lo — 'as the LORD had said through Moses to him.' The memorial formula closes the account: the bronze plating on the altar is a permanent exegetical object, visible to every worshipper approaching the altar, encoding the theological lesson in the most durable medium available — fired bronze from the instruments of judgment.
41: 'But the next day the whole congregation of Israel grumbled against Moses and Aaron.' The rebellion was yesterday; the memorial altar covering is freshly installed; and Israel grumbles again. The speed of the recurrence is part of the narrative's point: divine judgment does not produce lasting repentance in a people whose fundamental orientation is not transformed. The charge against Moses and Aaron is grotesque: 'You have killed the people of YHWH.' The rebels who challenged YHWH's order are reframed as YHWH's people killed by illegitimate authority — the moral inversion is complete.
42: When the congregation assembles against Moses and Aaron, they turn toward the tent of meeting and the cloud has covered it — the glory of YHWH appears again. The theophanic reappearance in direct response to fresh grumbling signals that YHWH takes the repeated rebellion as seriously as the original. The cloud covering the tent is a double sign: YHWH's presence is protective of the sanctuary and its authorized servants, and ominous toward those who challenge them.
43: Moses and Aaron go to the front of the tent of meeting — the appropriate response to a divine appearance: they present themselves at YHWH's threshold. The contrast with the congregation's behavior is sharp: when YHWH appears, Moses and Aaron approach; the congregation had assembled against Moses and Aaron but now face a more immediate presence. Their charge against Moses is about to be overtaken by a direct divine response.
44: A second 'Get away from this congregation so I may consume them in an instant' — the divine command repeats exactly from v.21. The repetition is significant: the same threat, the same formula, the same urgency. The pattern of divine judgment → intercession → modified outcome is being replayed. YHWH's patience is not inexhaustible, but the pattern of covenantal intercession remains operative. Moses and Aaron's response will again be to fall facedown in intercession.
45: 'Get away from this congregation so that I may consume them in an instant.' Moses and Aaron fall facedown — the intercessory posture. But now there is no time for extended prayer: the plague has already begun. The gap between divine command and divine action is closing. The narrative's urgency increases: intercession must be accompanied by action.
46: Moses's instruction to Aaron is immediate and specific: take the censer, add fire from the altar (not strange fire), put incense on it, go quickly to the congregation and make atonement. The censer Aaron is instructed to use carries the legitimate altar fire — the opposite of the 250's unauthorized fire. The same instrument that condemned the rebels (censer + incense) is now deployed by the authorized priest for the community's salvation. The irony is deliberate: the instrument whose misuse caused judgment is now, in authorized hands, the instrument of atonement.
47: Aaron runs into the middle of the congregation — vayarutz el-tokh haqqahal — the plague had already begun. The running signals urgency: Aaron is literally racing against the plague's spread. He makes atonement (yekhapper) for the people and stands between the dead and the living — bein hametim ubein hakhayim. This image of priestly mediation is one of the most powerful in the Torah: Aaron standing in the space between death and life, his censer creating an atoning boundary that the plague cannot cross.
48: 'He stood between the dead and the living, and the plague was stopped.' Vata'atzar hamaggerfah — the plague was restrained/stopped. The priestly act of intercession-through-incense halts a divine judgment already in progress. The image of standing between dead and living will echo through Israel's prophetic tradition (cf. Ezek 22:30: YHWH seeks someone to 'stand in the breach') and ultimately in the NT's presentation of Christ as the great high priest who 'always lives to intercede' (Heb 7:25).
49: 14,700 died in the plague — 'besides those who had died in the matter of Korah.' The precise number is given: YHWH's judgments are not vague but specific and bounded. The number records both the severity of the judgment (greater than many battles) and its limitation (it stopped). The 14,700 are a discrete, counted body — the plague's toll is inscribed in Israel's memory with the precision of a census.
50: Aaron returns to Moses at the entrance of the tent of meeting after the plague has stopped. The return to the tent — the site of YHWH's covenant presence — closes the episode. The entire sequence (rebellion → earth swallowing → fire → memorial altar covering → fresh grumbling → plague → priestly intercession → plague stopped) has been contained within the sanctuary's gravitational field: everything happens at or near the tent of meeting, and everything resolves at its entrance. The tent remains the center of Israel's covenantal life through all the turbulence.
